prompt: When strength drops during a cut, how can I tell whether it’s normal from the deficit or a sign I’m overreaching?
expected_output: Eric said some strength loss during aggressive leanness phases can be normal, especially deep in prep, but overreaching can mask performance and make losses look worse. His diagnostic move is to actually deload instead of pushing through: if bodyweight drops rapidly and strength rebounds after deloading, fatigue/overreaching was likely a major contributor. If nothing improves across a normal deload window, the drop may be more expected from the cut itself or from broader programming/nutrition issues that need adjustment. Greg added that strength athletes often catastrophize small losses while cutting to a lower class; relative metrics can give better context. One practical check he gave was tracking bodyweight pull-up/dip rep maxes over time: if bodyweight falls and reps are stable or rising, losses are likely proportionate; if both bodyweight and reps are dropping, losses may be disproportionately large. Overall, they recommended using deload response and relative-performance context before assuming the cut is failing.
topic: strength loss vs overreaching in a cut
assertions: Answer recommends deloading as a diagnostic tool to distinguish overreaching from normal deficit-related loss
Answer states if strength rebounds after deload, fatigue/overreaching was likely a contributor
Answer mentions tracking bodyweight pull-up/dip rep maxes as a practical check
Answer recommends using deload response and relative-performance context before assuming the cut is failing
